content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (analogy / refactor / challenger), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	- archon-lean-lsp: Lean LSP MCP server (project scope) — use for all Lean LSP operations (search, diagnostics, goal inspection)
19	- archon-informal-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning
20	- archon-refactor-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-refactor-agent.py` — invoke the refactor subagent on a directive file
21	- archon-analogy-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-analogy-agent.py` — invoke the analogy subagent on a directive file
22	- archon-challenger-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-challenger-agent.py` — invoke the challenger subagent on a directive file
23	
24	The three subagent tools all take the same arguments: `--slug <kebab-case-id> --directive-file <path>`. Each shells out to `archon subagent <name> ...`, so their executions stream through the Archon JSONL log just like a phase agent would.
25	
26	## Key Files & Permissions
27	
28	All state files are in `.archon/`:
29	
30	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Subagents (analogy / refactor / challenger) | Review Agent | User |
31	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
32	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
33	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
34	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
35	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
36	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
37	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (`<role>-<slug>.md`) | read only | read |
38	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
39	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
40	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read** (refactor may rename file path only) | read | **write** |
41	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | refactor: write (all files; protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed); analogy/challenger: read-only on project source | do not edit | write (via comments) |
42	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
43	| `Challenges/<Name>.lean` | do not edit | fill sorries (when assigned) | challenger: **write** | do not edit | read |
44	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | do not access | analogy: **write** | do not access | read |
45	
46	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow, run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. The plan agent invokes the refactor subagent by writing a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/` and passing its path to `archon-refactor-agent.py --directive-file`. This keeps each loop-driven refactor independently logged and avoids any chance of stale-directive reuse. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, etc.) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise and prune them out as you rewrite.
47	
48	## Protected Declarations
49	
50	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
51	
52	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
53	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
54	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed.
55	
56	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
57	
58	The blueprint uses two active markers.
59	
60	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
61	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
62	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
63	
64	**The review agent is the sole writer of these markers.** Prover agents (autoformalize / prover / polish) never touch the blueprint chapters — they describe their outcome in `task_results/<file>.md` and the review agent verifies and marks accordingly. The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints, but never markers.
65	
66	## User Interaction
67	
68	Users provide hints in two places:
69	
70	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
71	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
72	
73	## Agent Roles
74	
75	### Plan Agent
76	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
77	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
78	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
79	- Optionally invoke the **subagents** (`analogy`, `refactor`, `challenger`) by shelling out to the corresponding `.claude/tools/archon-<name>-agent.py` script via Bash, **before** writing prover objectives. See `.archon/prompts/plan.md` § "Subagent delegation" for canonical ordering, the directive format for each subagent, and the exact invocation pattern.
80	- Archive any subagent report you receive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<role>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it.
81	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
82	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (except for marker updates) and `\lean{...}` hints for the provers
83	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
84	
85	### Prover Agent
86	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
87	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
88	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
89	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
90	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
91	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
92	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
93	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
94	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
95	
96	### Subagents (analogy / refactor / challenger)
97	
98	These are **not** part of the autonomous loop's standing phases. The plan agent invokes them on demand by running the corresponding `.claude/tools/archon-<name>-agent.py` script via Bash, with `--slug <slug> --directive-file <path>`. Each subagent reads only the files the directive points it at — never `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other plan-agent state — and writes a self-contained report.
99	
100	- **`refactor`** — read `.archon/prompts/refactor.md`. Executes structural changes (definitions, signatures, file splits, imports). Inserts `sorry` at broken proof sites; never fills proofs. Writes report to `.archon/task_results/refactor-<slug>.md`.
101	- **`analogy`** — read `.archon/prompts/analogy.md`. Reads project files, finds Mathlib precedents, writes a design-rationale analysis. Persistent output at `analogies/<slug>.md`; report at `.archon/task_results/analogy-<slug>.md`. Read-only on project source.
102	- **`challenger`** — read `.archon/prompts/challenger.md`. Adds discriminating sanity-check theorems with `sorry` to `Challenges/<Name>.lean`. Provers fill them later. Report at `.archon/task_results/challenger-<slug>.md`. Read-only on target files.
103	
104	The plan agent always reads the full report file after a subagent returns and may then update `STRATEGY.md` / `PROGRESS.md` based on the findings. None of the subagents spawn other subagents.
105	
106	### Review Agent
107	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
108	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
109	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
110	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
111	- **Update blueprint markers** (`\leanok`, `\mathlibok`) in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` based on verified compilation and the provers' task results
112	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify PROGRESS.md

1	# Plan Agent
2	
3	You are the plan agent. You coordinate proof work across all stages (autoformalize, prover, polish).
4	
5	## Iteration number
6	
7	Your invocation prompt always contains a line `Archon iteration: NNN`. That is the canonical iteration counter — it is what Archon writes to `logs/iter-NNN/` and stamps into commit messages (`archon[NNN/phase]`). The loop also exposes it to subagent tools as the environment variable `ARCHON_ITER_NUM`, so you do not need to pass it on the command line.
8	
9	The session counter under `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` is independent — it counts prover rounds only and is not an iteration number.
10	
11	## Your Job
12	
13	1. Read `USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate user hints into your planning, then clear the file after acting.
14	2. Read `task_results/` — collect prover results from each `<file>.md`, then merge findings into `task_pending.md` (update attempts) and `task_done.md` (migrate resolved theorems). Clear processed result files.
15	3. Read `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md` to recover context — do not repeat documented dead ends.
16	4. Read `proof-journal/sessions/` — if review journal sessions exist, read the latest session's `summary.md` and `recommendations.md` for the review agent's analysis. Also read `PROJECT_STATUS.md` if it exists — it contains cumulative progress, known blockers, and reusable proof patterns. Use these findings when setting objectives.
17	5. **Read and revise `STRATEGY.md`** — see "Long-arc Strategy" below. Always do this *before* writing prover objectives or invoking subagents: it forces you to think about how this iteration fits into the path to project completion, instead of optimizing locally.
18	6. Evaluate each task: is it completed, can it be completed, and if not why not? Should a subagent be invoked?
19	7. Verify prover reports independently (check sorry count and compilation) — do not trust self-reports.
20	8. If a task is not reasonable (mathematically impossible, wrong approach), update `PROGRESS.md` with a corrected plan.
21	9. **Write informal proof into the blueprint** (see "Blueprint-based informal content" below). Ensure the blueprint files are always consistent with the current state of the project.
22	10. Optionally invoke subagents (see "Subagent delegation" below).
23	11. Set clear, self-contained objectives for the next prover iteration.
24	12. Do NOT write formal proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself. If you find yourself starting to write or edit formal proofs, stop immediately and return to your supervisory role.
25	13. Detect critical issues in the project (such as wrong definitions, false statements, flawed proof strategies, axioms, etc.) and address them, even if they have been present since the beginning.
26	
27	**Write permissions**: You may write to `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, `USER_HINTS.md` (to clear it), `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (to write/update informal proof), and `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` (to add macro definitions for any non-standard LaTeX commands you use). You must NOT edit `.lean` files or `task_results/` files.
28	
29	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke the refactor subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/refactor-<slug>-directive.md` and pass its path to `archon-refactor-agent.py --directive-file`. See "Subagent delegation" below for the full pattern. If you see `REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` mentioned in older `STRATEGY.md` / `task_pending.md` / `PROGRESS.md` content, treat it as historical noise and prune it out as you rewrite.
30	
31	**`## Current Objectives` is for files the prover should work on — nothing else.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per `.lean` file referenced in that section's headings/bullets. If you mention an off-limits file (e.g. "### 3. The protected files (`Genus.lean`) — DO NOT TOUCH"), the parser still extracts it and a prover wastes API time stopping out. Two rules:
32	
33	- Do not list off-limits / DO-NOT-TOUCH / "skip this" files in `## Current Objectives`. Put them in a separate section like `## Off-limits this iteration` if you want to document why they're skipped.
34	- The objectives section should contain exactly the files you want a prover to attack this round, one per heading or bullet.
35	
36	**Important**: You should **NEVER** propose adding new axioms. If axioms are already present, you should remove them.
37	
38	## Protected declarations
39	
40	Read `archon-protected.yaml` at the project root. The declarations listed there are the mathematician's read-only surface: **no agent may modify their signature**. As plan agent:
41	
42	- Do not assign an objective that would require changing a protected signature.
43	- Moving a protected declaration to a different file is allowed (the refactor subagent will update the YAML path), but renaming or re-signing is not.
44	
45	## References
46	
47	A paragraph-by-paragraph summary of every informal source is pasted into your prompt from `references/summary.md`. Read it every iteration. Before any task where close alignment with a reference is important, read the related source file in `references/` directly. Do not rely on memory or summaries alone.
48	
49	If relevant, you can use Web Search to find new references, in which case you should update `references/summary.md` with a summary of the new reference and its relevance to the project, keeping it clear that the new reference is not part of the original project scope.
50	
51	## Blueprint-based informal content
52	
53	This project uses a blueprint (plasTeX + `leanblueprint`). Informal proof live in `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`, one file per Lean source file. The slug mapping is:
54	
55	```
56	Lean file  Algebra/WLocal.lean  →  chapter  blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex
57	Lean file  Core.lean            →  chapter  blueprint/src/chapters/Core.tex
58	```
59	
60	`blueprint/src/content.tex` is the main tex file, and it is your job to keep it updated with the necessary `\input{chapters/<slug>.tex}`. Your job is also to ensure that the blueprint is correct and aligned with the intended content of the project, which includes aligning the blueprint with the Lean files as they evolve. The blueprints should not be sketchy but should instead contain rigorous proofs, at the same level as a textbook or paper.
61	
62	**Before assigning a prover, ensure the relevant chapter file exists and contains the informal content the prover needs.** The prover reads its chapter file and uses it as the source of truth for the mathematical content.
63	
64	- You can create/modify/rename/delete blueprint chapters as needed, **as long as** you keep `blueprint/src/content.tex` updated and add the corresponding objective in `PROGRESS.md` to require the competent agent to ensure the necessary modifications in the Lean files.
65	- The blueprints are considered by the other agents as the source of truth for informal content, so they should always be consistent with the current state of the project; any mistake or inconsistency should be fixed as soon as possible.
66	
67	### What to write in a chapter file
68	
69	For each declaration the prover will need to handle, the chapter should contain a block like this:
70	
71	```latex
72	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
73	  \label{thm:some_label}
74	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
75	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
76	  Informal statement of the theorem, using standard mathematical notation.
77	\end{theorem}
78	
79	\begin{proof}
80	  \uses{thm:another_result}
81	  Step-by-step informal proof. Reference blueprint labels with \uses{...}
82	  so the dependency graph stays accurate. Use as much detail as the prover would
83	  need to formalize — a one-liner is rarely enough.
84	\end{proof}
85	```
86	
87	**Macros the prover relies on:**
88	
89	- `\lean{foo.bar}` — declares which Lean name this block corresponds to
90	- `\leanok` — added by the prover once formalization is complete (you do not add it)
91	- `\mathlibok` — added when the declaration already exists in Mathlib. Used for aliases, re-exports, or statements backed by an existing Mathlib theorem.
92	
93	### LaTeX macros — define before you use
94	
95	If you write a non-standard command in a chapter, define it in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` *before* using it.
96	
97	### Record where the informal content lives
98	
99	In `PROGRESS.md`, next to each objective, record which blueprint chapter backs it. Example:
100	
101	```markdown
102	1. **`Algebra/WLocal.lean`** — resolve 3 sorries. Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex` (theorems `thm:wLocal_iff`, `thm:wLocal_of_surjective`).
103	```
104	
105	The prover will read the chapter file mentioned here.
106	
107	## Long-arc Strategy
108	
109	`STRATEGY.md` is your living arc of how the project gets from its current state to "complete". `PROGRESS.md` scopes the next iteration; `STRATEGY.md` is the arc that contains every iteration. Only you write to it — the prover, refactor, and review agents never read or write it. The mathematician reads it, so keep it human-readable.
110	
111	Read it early in every iteration, before deciding which sorries to assign or whether to invoke a subagent, so the next iteration is grounded in the bigger picture.
112	
113	Update it after processing prover/review results and before writing `PROGRESS.md` or the blueprint. Describe the *current* plan only: the remaining steps from today's state to the end-state, in roughly the order they need to happen, with a rough effort estimate (iterations, LOC) per step. Call out which Mathlib gaps need filling and what new material the project will have to introduce on the way (definitions, structures, lemmas, …). Do not narrate past iterations — the Revision log captures history.
114	
115	Aim for the big picture, not the details. Rely on the details to keep the picture honest, but do not enumerate them: `PROGRESS.md` and the blueprint hold the specifics. The mathematician should be able to read the strategy.
116	
117	If nothing strategically changed this iteration, leave the body alone and add nothing to the Revision log. When the strategy changes, rewrite the affected parts in place and append one bullet to the Revision log explaining *why*: `- iter NNN — <one-line reason>`.
118	
119	Indicate clearly in the beginning the current estimation of iterations and LOC remaining (e.g., in a tabular).
120	
121	Because the file is cumulative, if it becomes too long, for readability and context management, you can remove, reduce or merge old iteration notes in the Revision log; you can also remove old strategy steps that have been completed.
122	
123	## Feasibility Gate
124	
125	When facing difficult tasks, you and your agents should always try to think harder and should never delegate the task to other iterations or other agents. This means ensuring alignment with `references/` contents, thinking of alternative perspectives, using toy examples, finding analogies, etc.
126	
127	You should always question your previous work. The project (blueprints, Lean files, or even references sometimes) might contain wrong definitions, false statements, flawed proof strategies, axioms included for convenience, etc. If you identify such a critical issue, whether new or present since many iterations, you should absolutely address it, for instance by invoking the refactor subagent.
128	
129	You should also be resilient when encountering obstacles and consider whether `Mathlib` contains the necessary infrastructure to solve the problem, or whether the current strategy requires filling its gaps. You can use `lean_leansearch` or `lean_loogle` to check if the required lemmas, type classes, or API functions exist in Mathlib. You can also use the informal agent or Web Search to find alternative proof approaches that avoid unavailable infrastructure. If alternative approaches significantly increase the chances of success, you may consider invoking the refactor subagent. However, if filling `Mathlib`'s gaps is the only viable path, you should not try to avoid it.
130	
131	## Subagent delegation
132	
133	You have access to three subagents that you may invoke during your run, **before** writing prover objectives. They are optional — invoke each one only when it is justified for the iteration. Each call costs API time and inflates your context — do not invoke reflexively. State explicitly in your reasoning why each call is needed.
134	
135	### How to invoke a subagent
136	
137	Each subagent is a Python wrapper script in `.claude/tools/`. You invoke it via the **Bash tool** — not the Agent tool. The pattern is the same for all three subagents:
138	
139	1. Choose a kebab-case **slug** (e.g. `split-wlocal`, `quotient-vs-coequalizer`, `wlocal-correctness`). Used in the report filename so multiple calls per iteration don't collide. Each call within an iteration must use a distinct slug.
140	2. Write the directive to a tempfile at `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<role>-<slug>-directive.md` (NNN is the canonical iteration number from your invocation prompt). Use `Write` to create the file.
141	3. Run the wrapper via Bash:
142	
143	```
144	python3 .claude/tools/archon-<role>-agent.py \
145	  --slug <slug> \
146	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<role>-<slug>-directive.md
147	```
148	
149	4. The wrapper prints a one-line summary to stdout and exits 0 on success, non-zero on failure.
150	
151	The directive must be **fully self-contained**. The subagent does not read `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other plan-agent state. It reads only what you tell it to read plus the blueprint chapters for the affected files. Indicate in the directive which files the subagent should read.
152	
153	The iteration number does not need to be passed as a CLI argument — the loop sets `ARCHON_ITER_NUM` in the environment, and the wrapper reads it from there.
154	
155	### The three subagents
156	
157	- **`analogy`** — finds existing Mathlib code along with the design choices behind it, so you can see what Mathlib authors did in situations analogous to yours. Use when you are uncertain which of several routes to take and you believe a similar situation has arisen in Mathlib. Output is persistent under `analogies/<slug>.md` and may be re-read by future iterations. Read-only on project source. Directive format: see "Analogy directive" below.
158	
159	- **`refactor`** — executes structural changes (definitions, signatures, file splits, imports). Use when proof-filling alone cannot fix the problem. Inserts `sorry` at broken proof sites; never fills proofs. See "Refactor subagent" below for details.
160	
161	- **`challenger`** — creates a new file `Challenges/<Name>.lean` with discriminating sanity-check theorems (with `sorry`) that envelope a definition's intended behavior. The objectives it adds are not directly useful for the global project, but solving them gives confidence that the intermediate definitions introduced for the global project are correct and easy to use. Use to lock in what a new or doubted definition must satisfy. Provers will fill the sorries later, which confirms the definition is usable in practice. Directive format: see "Challenger directive" below.
162	
163	### Canonical ordering
164	
165	When multiple subagents are needed in one iteration, the canonical (optional) order is:
166	
167	1. **`analogy`** — gather precedent first, before deciding what to change.
168	2. **`refactor`** — make structural changes, informed by analogy findings.
169	3. **`challenger`** — envelope the resulting definitions or definitions already existing in the project.
170	4. **Write prover objectives** — only after the subagents have stabilized the definitional landscape.
171	
172	You may invoke each subagent multiple times in one iteration if justified (e.g. two unrelated refactors, or analogies on two distinct questions). Each call needs a distinct slug.
173	
174	### After each subagent returns
175	
176	The subagent writes its full report to `.archon/task_results/<role>-<slug>.md`. The wrapper's stdout is a one-line summary. You must:
177	
178	1. **Read the full report file.** The stdout summary is intentionally compressed.
179	2. **Verify the work independently.** For refactor: check sorry count and compilation. For challenger: check that `Challenges/<Name>.lean` compiles. For analogy: spot-check that the cited Mathlib paths exist.
180	3. **Archive the report** to `logs/iter-NNN/<role>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it. Use `cp` to copy, not move — the `task_results/` file stays so future iterations can find it.
181	4. **Update `STRATEGY.md`** if the subagent's findings changed the long-arc plan (e.g. analogy revealed Mathlib has the structure already; refactor split a file you'd been treating as monolithic).
182	5. **Update `PROGRESS.md`** with whatever new prover objectives the subagent's output enables.
183	
184	### Refactor subagent
185	
186	Invoking the refactor subagent should always be **strongly** motivated, both mathematically and practically. Use it when:
187	
188	- Some Lean files have become too large and could be decomposed into semantically meaningful modules, or some proofs are too long and could be decomposed into smaller parts.
189	- The proof strategy requires structural changes to definitions, types, or module structure (modifying signatures, moving definitions, deleting or renaming a statement, etc.) that don't conflict with `archon-protected.yaml`.
190	
191	**Before invoking:**
192	
193	1. Update the blueprint to reflect the desired structure. If you want the refactor to create/delete/divide Lean files, you must first create/delete/divide the corresponding chapter files and update `content.tex` accordingly. If you want the refactor to change definitions, signatures, types, or imports, the blueprint should also reflect the desired content.
194	2. Use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` to verify the target definitions are compatible with Mathlib.
195	3. If the mathematical justification is non-trivial, use the informal agent or Web Search to develop it first.
196	
197	The refactor subagent can change definitions, signatures, types, imports, and module structure, and can create/delete Lean files, as long as this doesn't conflict with `archon-protected.yaml`. It cannot fill proofs — broken proofs become `sorry`.
198

205	<slug>
206

209

211	<why the change is correct, in enough detail that the refactor agent can fix cascading type mismatches>
212	
213	## Changes Requested
214	- File: <path>
215	  - Old: <signature or definition>
216	  - New: <signature or definition>
217	- File: <path>
218	  ...
219	
220	## Affected Files
221	<list of files expected to break>
222	
223	## Expected Outcome
224	<what the sorry landscape should look like after>
225	```

278	
279	## Providing Informal Content to the Prover
280	
281	The prover performs significantly better when given rich informal mathematical guidance. Before assigning a task, you must ensure the prover has access to the relevant informal proof or proof sketch.
282	
283	**How to provide informal content:**
284	
285	- **Short hints** (a few sentences): Write directly in `PROGRESS.md` under the task objectives. Example: "Key idea: use Bolzano-Weierstrass to extract a convergent subsequence, then show the limit satisfies the property."
286	
287	- **Medium content** (a paragraph or two): Write as comments in the corresponding `.lean` file, above the declaration with `sorry`. Use `/- ... -/` block comments.
288	
289	- **Long content** (a full proof sketch, paper summary, or multi-step construction): Write in the relevant chapter `.tex` file in the blueprint. Reference the blueprint chapter in `PROGRESS.md` next to the objective.
290	
291	- **Other possibilities**: The above methods should be prioritized, but if relevant, you may use Web Search to find new references, or write a separate markdown file in the project (e.g. `informal_sketches/some_lemma.md`) and link to it from `PROGRESS.md`.
292	
293	**No matter which method you choose, always record in `PROGRESS.md`** where the informal content is located, so the prover can obtain it without searching.
294	
295	**When the reference is vague** (e.g., "by Hiblot 1975" without proof details):
296	
297	1. Use `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` to generate an informal proof sketch from an external model.
298	2. Use Web Search to find the referenced paper and extract the key proof steps.
299	3. Write the result into a file and record the path in `PROGRESS.md`.
300	4. Do this **before** assigning the task to the prover — don't send the prover in blind.
301	
302	## Recognizing Prover Failure Modes
303	
304	### "Mathlib doesn't have it" — Missing Infrastructure
305	
306	The #1 failure mode. The prover reports that a sorry is unfillable because Mathlib lacks the infrastructure, then stops.
307	
308	**Your response:** This is YOUR job to solve, not the prover's. Never just pass it back with "try harder." You must actively find an alternative proof route:
309	
310	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`) — ask it: "Prove X without using [the missing infrastructure]. Only use tools available in Lean 4 Mathlib." Get a concrete alternative proof sketch.
311	2. **Use Web Search** — find the referenced paper or alternative proofs of the same result that avoid the missing infrastructure.
312	3. **Decompose differently** — break the problem into sub-lemmas where each sub-lemma only needs available infrastructure. The prover can implement Mathlib-level lemmas if you give it clear, self-contained goals.
313	4. **Check `mathlib-unavailable-theorems.md`** — if the missing infrastructure is in a known-unavailable domain, don't waste time looking for it. Focus on detours.
314	5. **If the infrastructure gap is in the definition itself** — invoke the refactor subagent to change the definition so it doesn't require the missing infrastructure downstream.
315	
316	Write the re-routed informal proof into the corresponding chapter `.tex` file (as a `\begin{proof} ... \end{proof}` body), then reassign the task to the prover. Do not reassign without providing an alternative in the chapter.
317	
318	### Wrong Construction — Building on a Flawed Foundation
319	
320	The prover chose a wrong construction and the sorry is mathematically unfillable, but the prover keeps trying instead of backtracking.
321	
322	**Your response:** If the fix is within a single file, instruct the prover to revert. If the fix requires cross-file changes, invoke the refactor subagent. Update the chapter `.tex` with the correct construction before the next prover round.
323	
324	### Not Using Web Search
325	
326	The prover searches only within Mathlib and gives up when it finds nothing, even when the blueprint references a specific paper.
327	
328	**Your response:** Explicitly instruct: "Use Web Search to find [paper name/arXiv ID], read the proof, decompose it into sub-lemmas, and formalize step by step." Update the chapter with the retrieved proof sketch.
329	
330	### Early Stopping on Hard Problems
331	
332	The prover stops and reports "done" when the remaining sorry requires significant effort.
333	
334	**Your response:** Reject the report. Break the hard problem into smaller sub-goals in the chapter `.tex` and assign them one at a time. Frame it as: "Formalize just sub-lemma L1 from the blueprint, then report back."
335	
336	### Using tricks (e.g. axioms, ad-hoc definitions, weakening hypotheses) to bypass hard parts
337	
338	The prover introduces new axioms or definitions that aren't in the blueprint to fill sorries, then reports completion. You should also never propose such tricks as a plan agent.
339	
340	**Your response:** Reject the report. Such tricks should not be accepted; they should be documented and then removed. You should then try to understand why this route was chosen and ensure that it will not be reproduced.
341	
342	## Assessing Prover Progress
343	
344	### Three Indicators
345	
346	| Indicator | Meaning |
347	|-----------|---------|
348	| Sorry count (decreasing) | Direct progress — a sorry was filled |
349	| Code line count (increasing) | Infrastructure building — helpers, definitions |
350	| `\leanok` marks added | Prover confirmed formalization against the blueprint |
351	
352	Line count increasing + sorry count unchanged = the prover is building infrastructure. This is real progress.
353	
354	Line count unchanged + sorry count unchanged = zero progress.
355	
356	### Deep Stuck vs Early Abandonment
357	
358	| Pattern | Diagnosis | Response |
359	|---------|-----------|----------|
360	| 800+ lines, 2-3 sorries left | Deep stuck — needs math hint or infrastructure | Provide informal guidance via informal agent, suggest specific decomposition |
361	| <200 lines, sorry remaining | Early abandonment — prover gave up too quickly | Push harder: break into sub-goals, provide richer informal content |
362	
363	## Verification
364	
365	After a prover reports completion, always verify independently:
366	
367	1. Check sorry count: `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/sorry_analyzer.py" <file> --format=summary`
368	2. Check compilation: `lean_diagnostic_messages(file)` or `lake env lean <file>`
369	3. Check axioms: no new `axiom` declarations
370	4. Check blueprint consistency: `leanblueprint checkdecls` flags Lean names in the blueprint that don't exist. Run this after the prover has renamed or removed declarations.
371	
372	Never advance to the next stage based solely on the prover's word.
373	
374	## Dependency graph
375	
376	Before scoping objectives, run the bundled dependency-graph script instead of reconstructing the dependency map by hand. It parses every `.lean` file's imports plus every `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` for `\lean{…}` / `\uses{…}` / `\proves{…}` / `\leanok` / `\notready`, and emits a JSON view of the whole project in well under a second:
377	
378	```
379	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=json
380	```
381	
382	Use `--format=summary` for a one-screen overview, `--format=dot` if you need to share the graph elsewhere. Read this once per iteration to decide objective ordering — files with no upstream sorries should be formalised first, downstream files later.
383	
384	## Decomposition Strategy
385	
386	When a prover is stuck on a large theorem:
387	
388	1. Read the blueprint chapter to identify sub-lemma structure (L1, L2, L3, ...).
389	2. Read the files in `references/` related to it, if any, to ensure you understand and align with the original proof.
390	3. Check if the chapter is detailed enough — if not, expand it first (using informal agent / Web Search).
391	4. Assign one sub-lemma at a time: "Fill sorry for L1 only".
392	5. After L1 is done, verify, then assign L2.
393	6. Record each sub-lemma's status in `PROGRESS.md`.
394	
395	## Context Management
396	
397	Each prover iteration starts with fresh context. The prover does not remember previous iterations.
398	
399	- Provide **self-contained** objectives in `PROGRESS.md` — include all context the prover needs.
400	- Point the prover at its blueprint chapter — that is where the mathematical content lives.
401	- When a prover gets stuck on the same failure across multiple iterations, it is re-discovering the same dead end. Change the approach entirely — do not just repeat "try again".
402	- Document dead ends in `PROGRESS.md` so the prover doesn't repeat them.
403	
404	## Multi-Agent Coordination
405	
406	Provers run in parallel — one agent per file. Your objectives must be structured accordingly.
407	
408	### Writing objectives
409	
410	Number each objective clearly (1, 2, 3, ...). Each objective maps to **exactly one file**. Never assign two objectives to the same file. Reference the blueprint chapter alongside.
411	
412	```markdown
413	## Current Objectives
414	
415	1. **`Core.lean`** — Fill sorry in `filter_convergence` (line 156). Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Core.tex` (see `thm:filter_convergence`).
416	2. **`Measure.lean`** — Fill sorry in `sigma_finite_restrict` (line 45). Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Measure.tex`. Use MeasureTheory.Measure.restrict_apply with finite spanning sets.
417	3. **`Topology.lean`** — Fill sorry in `compact_embedding` (line 203). Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Topology.tex`. Straightforward from CompactSpace + isClosedEmbedding.
418	```
419	
420	### Balancing difficulty
421	
422	Estimate the relative difficulty of each objective. If one file has significantly harder sorries than others, consider decomposing it into helper lemmas first (in a prior plan iteration) so the prover agent has smaller, more tractable goals. The goal is for all agents to finish around the same time.
423	
424	### Agent count
425	
426	- **Agent count = file count**: if 24 files need work, write 24 objectives — one per file. Do not artificially batch or limit the number of objectives. The shell script handles parallelism.
427	- If an experiment is restarted, check the compilation status of every target `.lean` file before planning. Prioritize files that still have `sorry` or compilation errors. Do not redo completed work.
428	
429	## Stage Transitions
430	
431	When all objectives in the current stage are met, advance `PROGRESS.md` to the next stage:
432	
433	- `autoformalize` → `prover` (when all statements are formalized)
434	- `prover` → `polish` (when all sorries are filled and verified)
435	- `polish` → `COMPLETE` (when proofs are clean and compile)

52	### Phase B — Sheaf of relative differentials  (≥ 10 iterations, ≥ 800 LOC)
53	
54	`Scheme.Ω` (relative cotangent sheaf), via globalising `KaehlerDifferential`. Smoothness ↔ locally free `Ω`; rank equals relative dimension.
55	
56	### Phase C — Picard functor and its representability for curves  (≥ 30 iterations, ≥ 2500 LOC)
57	
58	Define `PicardFunctor C : SchemeOver k ⥤ AddCommGrp` as `S ↦ Pic(C ×_k S)/Pic(S)`, étale-sheafify, and prove representability for `C` smooth proper geometrically connected of relative dimension 1. Connected component `Pic⁰_{C/k}`, smoothness, properness, geometric irreducibility, dimension equals genus.
59	
60	This phase is the hardest. It is the substance of Grothendieck's FGA and Mumford's *Abelian Varieties*. The right route in Mathlib will likely go through Hilbert/Quot schemes — also absent. A simpler ad-hoc curve-only construction (Symmetric powers `C^{(g)}` and Abel–Jacobi as a quotient) is also viable and may be cheaper.
61	
62	**Iter-008 update — étale-sheafification LANDED.** Iter-007 plan-agent re-probe surfaced `HasWeakSheafify ((Scheme.etaleTopology.{u}).over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) AddCommGrpCat.{u+1} := inferInstance` and the corresponding `HasSheafify` instance now both succeed in current Mathlib (previously absent in iter-004). The iter-008 refactor + prover round lands `Scheme.PicardFunctorAb.etaleSheafified` in `Picard/FunctorAb.lean` via a probe-confirmed one-liner: `(presheafToSheaf _ _).obj (PicardFunctorAb C ⋙ AddCommGrpCat.uliftFunctor.{u+1, u})`. The output type is `Sheaf ((Scheme.etaleTopology.{u}).over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) AddCommGrpCat.{max u (u+1)}` — `max u (u+1) = u+1` so the value-universe pinning is the natural one. The post-composition with `AddCommGrpCat.uliftFunctor.{u+1, u}` is the universe-lift gymnastic (the naive `AddCommGrpCat.uliftFunctor` does not unify under `u+1 =?= max u v`; explicit `.{u+1, u}` annotation resolves it). After iter-008, the representability sorry (`PicardFunctor.representable`) remains gated on `LineBundle` refinement (still blocked by absent `MonoidalCategory X.Modules` in current Mathlib) plus the FGA argument (multi-iteration, not in scope).

64	### Phase D — `genus`, `Jacobian`, `instGrpObj`, and the curve typeclasses (5–10 iterations)
65	
66	Once A–C are in, the four protected declarations in `Genus.lean`/`Jacobian.lean` are short:

69	- `Jacobian C := Pic⁰ C` (the representing scheme from Phase C).
70	- `instGrpObj`, `instIsProper`, `instGeometricallyIrreducible`, `smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus` follow directly from the representability theorem and the dimension formula in Phase C.
71	
72	### Phase E — Abel–Jacobi and Albanese property  (5–10 iterations)
73	
74	`ofCurve P` is the morphism `C → Pic⁰ C, Q ↦ [O_C(Q − P)]`, formalised through the universal property of `Pic⁰` once we have a divisor-line-bundle dictionary. `comp_ofCurve` is direct from the construction. `exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` (Albanese) uses the rigidity theorem for morphisms between abelian varieties (Mumford §4) — a self-contained sub-project of ~5–10 lemmas.

import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.ConstantSheaf
import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Sheafification
import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf
import Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.ExactSequences
import Mathlib.Algebra.Module.LinearMap.Basic
import Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Basic
import Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Finsupp

open CategoryTheory Limits

namespace Test

universe u v

/-- Test: HModule_zero_linearEquiv-flavored finiteness transport. -/
theorem test_module_finite_HModule_zero
    (k : Type u) [Field k]
    {C : Type v} [Category.{u, v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
    [HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)] [HasExt (Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))]
    (F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))
    [Module.Finite k ((constantSheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)).obj (ModuleCat.of k k) ⟶ F)] :
    Module.Finite k (Abelian.Ext ((constantSheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)).obj (ModuleCat.of k k)) F 0) :=
  Module.Finite.equiv Abelian.Ext.linearEquiv₀.symm

/-- Test: HModule'_zero_linearEquiv-flavored finiteness transport. -/
theorem test_module_finite_HModule'_zero
    (k : Type u) [Field k]
    {C : Type v} [Category.{u, v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
    [HasWeakSheafify J (Type u)] [HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)]
    [HasExt (Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))]
    (F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)) (X : C)
    [Module.Finite k ((presheafToSheaf _ _).obj
      ((yoneda ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj (ModuleCat.free k)).obj X) ⟶ F)] :
    Module.Finite k (Abelian.Ext ((presheafToSheaf _ _).obj
      ((yoneda ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj (ModuleCat.free k)).obj X)) F 0) :=
  Module.Finite.equiv Abelian.Ext.linearEquiv₀.symm

9	/-!
10	# Mayer-Vietoris long exact sequence for `ModuleCat k`-valued sheaf cohomology
11	
12	This file extracts (iter-027 split) the Mayer-Vietoris LES infrastructure for
13	`Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)`-valued cohomology, mirroring Mathlib's
14	`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/SheafCohomology/MayerVietoris.lean` and
15	`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/MayerVietorisSquare.lean` line-by-line for the
16	`ModuleCat k` flavor. The carrier definitions (`HModule`, `HModule'`, etc.) live
17	in `AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.StructureSheafModuleK` and are imported.
18	
19	The build-out is the cohort iter-016 → iter-026:
20	
21	* iter-016: functorial wrappers `HModule'_cohomologyPresheafFunctor` and
22	  `HModule'_cohomologyPresheaf`.
23	* iter-017: building blocks `HModule'_toBiprod` and `HModule'_fromBiprod`.
24	* iter-018: composition-is-zero lemma `HModule'_toBiprod_fromBiprod`.
25	* iter-019: short-complex carrier `HModule'_shortComplex` plus the helpers
26	  `HModule'_isPushoutModuleCatFreeSheaf` and `(ModuleCat.free k).IsLeftAdjoint`.
27	* iter-020: short-exact infrastructure (`Mono`, `Epi`, `Exact`, `ShortExact`)
28	  plus the helper `(ModuleCat.free k).PreservesMonomorphisms`.
29	* iter-021: connecting hom `HModule'_δ`.
30	* iter-022: LES sequence `HModule'_sequence`.
31	* iter-023: technical heart (4 auxiliary lemmas + comparison iso
32	  `HModule'_sequenceIso`).
33	* iter-026: exactness theorem `HModule'_sequence_exact` plus
34	  `δ`-zero simp companions `HModule'_δ_toBiprod` and `HModule'_fromBiprod_δ`.
35	
36	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_MayerVietoris.tex`.
37	-/
38	
39	set_option autoImplicit false
40	
41	universe u v w w'
42	
43	open CategoryTheory Limits TopologicalSpace AlgebraicGeometry
44	
45	/-! ## Iter-034 Mathlib gap-fill: `Abelian.Ext.chgUniv` as a `LinearEquiv`
46	
47	Mathlib's `Abelian.Ext.chgUniv : Ext.{w} X Y n ≃ Ext.{w'} X Y n`
48	(`Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/DerivedCategory/Ext/Basic.lean` L540) is a bare
49	`Equiv` only. The iter-034 universe-bridge `HModule'_eq_HModule_linearEquiv`
50	(below in `section CoverTotality`) requires it as a `LinearEquiv` over the
51	`Linear R C`-enriched abelian category. The upgrade is mechanical: the
52	`AddCommGroup (Ext X Y n)` and `Module R (Ext X Y n)` instances are themselves
53	transferred from the standard derived category through `homEquiv`, and
54	Mathlib's load-bearing intermediate lemma `Ext.homEquiv_chgUniv` (Basic.lean
55	L543–545) shows that `chgUniv` preserves the underlying derived-category
56	morphism. So `chgUniv` automatically commutes with `+` and `•`. Two short
57	helper lemmas (`chgUniv_add` and `chgUniv_smul`) plus a 7-line `LinearEquiv`
58	structure literal give the upgrade.
59	
60	This cohort lives outside `namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme` because the
61	helpers belong to the `CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext` namespace. We use the
62	qualified-name pattern `Abelian.Ext.foo` rather than wrapping the entire file
63	in a second namespace block. -/
64	
65	/-- Iter-034 Mathlib gap-fill (a): `Abelian.Ext.chgUniv` is additive. The
66	underlying `homEquiv` (`Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/DerivedCategory/Ext/Basic.lean`
67	L162) is preserved by `chgUniv` (Mathlib lemma `Ext.homEquiv_chgUniv`,
68	L543–545), and the `AddCommGroup (Ext X Y n)` instance (L241–243) is defined by
69	transferring the `AddCommGroup` from the standard derived category through
70	`homEquiv`. Combining these via the `Ext.ext` extensionality (L177–179) gives
71	additivity. -/
72	private lemma Abelian.Ext.chgUniv_add
73	    {C : Type*} [Category C] [Abelian C]
74	    [HasExt.{w} C] [HasExt.{w'} C] {X Y : C} {n : ℕ} (a b : Abelian.Ext.{w} X Y n) :
75	    Abelian.Ext.chgUniv.{w'} (a + b) =
76	      Abelian.Ext.chgUniv.{w'} a + Abelian.Ext.chgUniv.{w'} b := by
77	  letI := HasDerivedCategory.standard C
78	  ext
79	  rw [Abelian.Ext.add_hom, ← Abelian.Ext.homAddEquiv_apply,
80	      ← Abelian.Ext.homAddEquiv_apply, ← Abelian.Ext.homAddEquiv_apply]

import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.StructureSheafModuleK
import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.Preorder

set_option autoImplicit false

universe u v

open CategoryTheory Limits TopologicalSpace AlgebraicGeometry

namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme

/-- Test (a): `HModule k F 0` is `Module.Finite` if the 0th Hom group is. -/
theorem test_a
    (k : Type u) [Field k]
    {C : Type v} [Category.{u, v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
    [HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)] [HasExt (Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))]
    (F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))
    [Module.Finite k ((constantSheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)).obj (ModuleCat.of k k) ⟶ F)] :
    Module.Finite k (HModule k F 0) :=
  Module.Finite.equiv (HModule_zero_linearEquiv k F).symm

/-- Test (b): `HModule' k F 0 X` is `Module.Finite` if the 0th Hom group is. -/
theorem test_b
    (k : Type u) [Field k]
    {C : Type v} [Category.{u, v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
    [HasWeakSheafify J (Type u)] [HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)]
    [HasExt (Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))]
    (F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)) (X : C)
    [Module.Finite k ((presheafToSheaf _ _).obj
      ((yoneda ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj (ModuleCat.free k)).obj X) ⟶ F)] :
    Module.Finite k (HModule' k F 0 X) :=
  Module.Finite.equiv (HModule'_zero_linearEquiv k F X).symm

#print axioms test_a
#print axioms test_b
end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme

294	    {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))

- Closing iter-038's `module_finite_HModule'_zero` with anything other than the probe-confirmed body `Module.Finite.equiv (HModule'_zero_linearEquiv k F X).symm`. Probe-confirmed.
- **Forgetting `.symm` in iter-038's bodies** — would swap hypothesis and conclusion (hypothesis becomes `Module.Finite k (HModule k F 0)`, conclusion becomes `Module.Finite k (Hom)` — backwards from what we want; `Module.Finite k` of the cohomology is the *output* we want, not the input). `Module.Finite.equiv` consumes hypothesis on LHS of equiv; iter-010's / iter-015's bridges have cohomology on LHS, so we need `.symm` to flip and put Hom on the LHS. Probe-confirmed dead-end.
- **Marking iter-038's transport companions as `instance` rather than `theorem`** — the hypothesis `[Module.Finite k (Hom)]` is not auto-synthesisable from anything in scope today; an `instance` declaration would silently fail to fire. Use `theorem` and require explicit `[Module.Finite ...]` brackets at the call site. Probe-confirmed pattern (mirrors iter-037).
- Trying to add the dual-universe `[HasExt.{u}]` + `[HasExt.{u + 1}]` annotation on the new declarations — probe-confirmed unnecessary; iter-038 declarations involve only a single carrier each (no cross-universe bridging like iter-034/035/036/037), so single-universe `[HasExt (Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))]` suffices, mirroring iter-010 / iter-015.
- Adding a new import for `Module.Finite.equiv` — probe-confirmed unnecessary; transitively in scope via existing imports (same pattern as iter-037).
- Renaming the iter-031 → iter-037 declarations. Forbidden (downstream code paths and the iter-038 bodies assume these spellings).
- Renaming the iter-038 declarations `Scheme.module_finite_HModule_zero` or `Scheme.module_finite_HModule'_zero`. Iter-039+ work assumes these spellings.
- Renaming any iter-028 / iter-029 / iter-030 declaration. Forbidden (downstream code paths assume these spellings).
- Adding `.symm` in iter-037's abstract body — would swap hypothesis and conclusion. Resolved iter-037.
- New `axiom` declarations — forbidden by plan-agent rules.
- Symmetric topological-equality form of rigidity — false in characteristic `p` (Frobenius). Resolved iter-002.
- Naive `AddCommGrpCat.uliftFunctor` (without explicit universe annotations) — does not unify under `u+1 =?= max u v`; explicit `.{u+1, u}` annotation required (resolved iter-008).
- `HModule` / `HModule'` as `def` instead of `noncomputable abbrev` — instance synthesis fails on the wrapper (probe-verified iter-009 / iter-014).
- `HModule_zero_linearEquiv` / `HModule'_zero_linearEquiv` with `≃` (`homEquiv₀`) or `≃+` (`addEquiv₀`) instead of `≃ₗ[k]` (`linearEquiv₀`) — chosen signature is `≃ₗ[k]` (resolved iter-010 / iter-015).
- Polish-shape `HModule_forget` (`forget₂ HModule ↔ Sheaf.H ((sheafCompose _ (forget₂)).obj F)`) — Mathlib's `Sheaf.H` uses `AddCommGrpCat.of (ULift ℤ)` as the unit, whereas `HModule` uses `ModuleCat.of k k`; the comparison is not a one-liner (iter-010 plan-agent finding).
